critic_raw:
The methodology is thoughtfully constructed and avoids several common pitfalls (temporal split, preprocessing fit on train only, composite label to reduce click bias). My analysis surfaces a few genuine structural issues — not manufacturing concerns, but real mechanisms of failure.

**Issue 1: Session-level engagement aggregate as an interaction feature leaks future within-session information**

The claim: 'session-level engagement aggregate summarizing article dwell and scroll activity within the current session' is listed as an interaction feature used to train the ranker.

The failure mechanism: At serving time, the ranker must score articles *before* the user reads them — the session hasn't happened yet. If the session-level engagement aggregate aggregates dwell/scroll from articles *already consumed in the session* prior to the current ranking decision, it is a valid causal feature. But as described, it is ambiguous whether it includes signals from the article being scored (i.e., the dwell/scroll on article X is used as a feature when scoring article X). If the latter, this is a form of target leakage — dwell on article X is a direct component of the composite relevance label for article X. Even a partial correlation between the aggregate and the per-article dwell component of the label would inflate training performance and make the model undeployable as described.

The document says 'helps distinguish shallow clicks from substantive consumption,' which is the exact language used to justify incorporating dwell into the label — suggesting this feature and the label are measuring the same underlying signal.

Evidence that would resolve this: Examine whether the engagement aggregate for item i in position p includes dwell/scroll on item i itself. If it does, remove item i's own signals from the aggregate and re-evaluate. A large performance drop on validation would confirm leakage was occurring.

**Issue 2: Historical engagement metrics on articles are computed from 'prior months' data' — the boundary is underspecified and may create leakage at article level**

The claim: Article features (historical CTR, historical resolution rate) are computed from prior months' data and are safe from leakage.

The failure mechanism: The temporal split places months 1–3 in training, month 4 in validation, months 5–6 in test. If historical engagement metrics for test-period articles are recomputed using all available data at the time of evaluation (i.e., including months 1–4 for articles appearing in test), that is fine. But if the same precomputed engagement metrics are used uniformly across all splits — specifically if test-period article stats were computed using signals from months 4–5 to 'fill in' articles with little history — then future information bleeds into the article feature for the test set. The phrase 'computed from prior months' data' does not specify *prior relative to what*, making this ambiguous.

For articles that appear first in validation or test (new content, which the design explicitly includes to test generalization), their historical stats would be zero or near-zero at the actual training cutoff. If those stats are enriched using later data, the model is evaluated under conditions it won't face at deployment.

Evidence: Check whether article-level engagement stats are time-indexed — i.e., for a query session in month 5, the article's historical CTR is computed only from sessions prior to month 5. If stats are computed once over the full 6-month window and then joined to all splits, this is a data leak.

**Issue 3: Composite label mixes resolution intent with article quality in a way that systematically disadvantages new articles**

The claim: The composite score (click + long dwell + no subsequent ticket) is a better proxy for relevance than clicks alone.

The failure mechanism: The 'no support ticket opened within 24 hours' (+1 point) component attributes ticket suppression to the article, but a user might not open a ticket because they gave up, because the issue resolved itself, or because they called support instead. More importantly, new articles — which the experiment explicitly includes to test generalization — have shorter exposure histories and are more likely to be presented to users with complex problems (since established articles already handle simpler queries). This means new articles face a structurally harder ticket-suppression task, systematically scoring lower on the composite label not because they are less relevant but because of selection effects in who sees them. The model then learns to rank new articles lower, which is the opposite of a desired generalization property.

This is not a fatal flaw on its own, but the held-out manual label comparison (~10% of queries) is the right instrument to detect this — yet the design doesn't state that this comparison is stratified by article age. Without that stratification, the manual label audit won't reveal the systematic bias against new articles.

Evidence: Stratify the implicit vs. explicit label comparison by article age quartile. If newer articles consistently score lower on the composite label relative to their manually assigned relevance, the label is systematically biased against new content.

**Issue 4: DistilBERT baseline is not BM25 + behavioral, so the comparison doesn't isolate the value of behavioral signals**

The claim: 'The comparison isolates behavioral features and ranking optimization.'

The failure mechanism: The proposed model is LightGBM using BM25 + semantic similarity + behavioral features. The baseline is DistilBERT cosine similarity alone. This is a two-variable comparison: the proposed model differs from the baseline in (a) having a learning-to-rank objective and (b) having behavioral features. Any observed gain could come from either. A BM25 + behavioral LightGBM ranker without the semantic similarity feature — or a DistilBERT retriever reranked by a simple CTR model — would isolate which component drives the improvement. Without these intermediate ablations, the conclusion 'behavioral features improve ranking' cannot be cleanly drawn from the comparison as designed.

Evidence: Add a LightGBM ranker trained on BM25 + behavioral features only (no semantic similarity) as an ablation baseline. If it performs near the full model, the semantic component is doing little. If the full model substantially outperforms the DistilBERT baseline but not this ablation, the behavioral features are the driver. Currently there is no way to distinguish these scenarios.

**Summary:**
Issues 1 and 2 are potential data integrity problems with clear resolution criteria. Issue 3 is a label construction bias that the existing audit design is not structured to catch. Issue 4 is a baseline design gap that prevents clean causal attribution. Issues 1 and 2 are the most structurally concerning because they could produce inflated test metrics without any visible signal that something is wrong.
all_issues_raised: The session-level engagement aggregate may include the scored article's own dwell/scroll signals, creating direct target leakage since dwell is also a component of the composite relevance label.

Historical article engagement metrics are described as computed from 'prior months' data' without specifying prior relative to which split boundary, potentially enriching test-set article features with future information.

The 'no support ticket' label component systematically disadvantages new articles due to selection effects in who sees them, and the manual label audit is not stratified by article age to detect this bias.

The proposed model and baseline differ on two axes simultaneously (learning-to-rank objective and behavioral features), so the comparison cannot isolate the contribution of behavioral signals without intermediate ablations.

The session-level engagement aggregate encodes dwell and scroll data from the current session, which is unavailable at ranking time and partially overlaps with the label construction, creating direct lookahead leakage.
Historical article engagement metrics (CTR, resolution rate) are not precisely bounded to pre-training-window data, allowing within-training-window popularity trends to contaminate article features.
The 'no support ticket' label component is subject to position bias from the production ranker that generated the logs, and no propensity correction is applied to the label construction.

BM25 IDF is computed against the training corpus, systematically miscalibrating interaction scores for new articles that appear only in validation/test, undermining the stated goal of testing new-content generalization.
The session-level engagement aggregate may encode current-session dwell/resolution signal that constitutes label leakage and will be unavailable at serving time.
Article-level historical engagement metrics (CTR, resolution rate) may be computed globally rather than temporally bounded per split, leaking future engagement data into earlier windows.
The no-ticket resolution signal is session-level but attributed per-article, causing label ambiguity in multi-article sessions that conflates co-occurrence with resolution with causation.

The DistilBERT baseline is evaluated on behavioral labels it was not optimized for, making it impossible to determine whether the proposed model's gain comes from behavioral features or from label-metric alignment artifacts.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured design with several genuine strengths: temporal splitting, preprocessing fit on training only, multi-signal composite labels, and explicit confound stratification. The critique below focuses on structural issues rather than manufactured concerns.

**Issue 1: The session-level engagement aggregate creates lookahead leakage**

Claim: 'a session-level engagement aggregate summarizing article dwell and scroll activity within the current session' is a valid interaction feature.

Why it's wrong: Dwell time and scroll activity within the current session are only observable *after* the user has already interacted with the article. When the ranker is deployed, it must rank articles *before* the user clicks anything. This feature is not available at inference time for any article that hasn't yet been viewed in the session, and for articles already viewed it encodes post-hoc outcome information. If used to rank the first article presented, the feature is undefined or zero. If used to re-rank mid-session, it partially encodes the label (dwell ≥ 90s is one of the three label components). This isn't a distribution shift concern — it's a direct form of label leakage at training time that inflates NDCG@5 during evaluation, because test sessions also have this signal computed from the same session where the label was generated. The mechanism: a session where a user dwelt long and didn't open a ticket will have a high engagement aggregate AND a high label score; the model learns a spurious correlation that doesn't exist at serving time.

Evidence: Compare NDCG@5 with vs. without the session-level engagement aggregate feature on the test set. If removing it causes a large drop, leakage is operating. More directly, simulate inference-time conditions by zeroing the feature for all positions ≥ 1 in each session (as it would be unknown pre-click) and retest.

**Issue 2: Historical engagement metrics on articles are computed from 'prior months' data' — the boundary is underspecified relative to the split**

Claim: Article historical CTR and resolution rate are computed from prior months' data and are safe from leakage.

Why it might be wrong: The design says historical engagement metrics are 'computed from prior months' data' but does not specify whether 'prior months' means prior to the training window (i.e., pre-month-1) or prior to each respective data point's timestamp within the dataset. If historical CTR for an article used in month-3 training includes any data from months 1–3 (rolling or cumulative), then articles that appeared in months 5–6 test sessions and were also in training sessions will have CTR estimates that incorporate training-window behavior. This isn't catastrophic leakage, but it means the article features are not strictly pre-training-window statistics and could encode popularity trends that differ between train and test periods. More concretely: an article that surged in month 3 will have a high CTR feature in the test window, but this surge was caused by events in the training window — the model may over-fit to popularity as a proxy for relevance.

Evidence: Document exactly which date range is used to compute historical engagement metrics for each article. Then ablate: use only pre-month-1 article stats and measure the performance delta. If the model degrades substantially, it is relying on in-window popularity signals that would not generalize to newly published articles (which have no historical engagement at all).

**Issue 3: The composite label conflates resolution with article quality in a position-biased environment**

Claim: 'no support ticket opened within 24 hours' (+1 point) is a meaningful signal of article relevance.

Why it might be wrong: Ticket non-opening is confounded by at least two factors independent of article quality: (1) issue urgency — some users open tickets regardless of article quality because their problem requires human intervention; (2) article position and presentation — users who receive high-ranked results are more likely to find something and not ticket, creating a feedback loop where high-position articles accumulate more +1 ticket-avoidance points, making historical resolution rate (an article feature) encode prior position rather than content quality. The design notes position/popularity bias as a confound but addresses it only via stratification in secondary metrics, not in the label construction itself. This means the training label itself is corrupted by position bias in the production logs, and the model may learn to predict 'what did the old ranker show first' rather than 'what is genuinely helpful.'

Evidence: Compute, per article, the correlation between historical average rank position (at impression time in training data) and its resolution rate feature. If highly ranked articles have substantially higher resolution rates controlling for category and topic, position bias is operating in the label. An inverse propensity score correction on the label construction would be the fix; its absence is an architectural gap.

**Issue 4: BM25 term frequency computed against training corpus introduces a distributional mismatch for test queries**

Claim: BM25 TF computed against the training corpus is a valid query feature and interaction feature.

Why it might be wrong: BM25 IDF weights are corpus-dependent. New articles added in months 5–6 are not in the training corpus, so their term frequencies relative to IDF computed on months 1–3 articles are miscalibrated. For queries in the test window that retrieve these new articles, the BM25 interaction score between query and article will be computed using IDF from a corpus that doesn't include the article — this is not leakage, but it is a systematic mismatch that will suppress BM25 scores for new content and cause the model to underrank genuinely relevant new articles. The design explicitly says 'Articles appearing only in later windows are included in validation/test to test generalization to new content,' but this is precisely where the BM25 feature will be most degraded.

Evidence: Stratify NDCG@5 on the test set by article age (new articles appearing only in months 5–6 vs. articles present in training). If new articles show substantially worse NDCG despite being manually judged as relevant (using the held-out manual label set), the BM25 corpus mismatch is the most likely explanation.

**Summary:** Issue 1 (session engagement aggregate leakage) is the most critical because it directly inflates evaluation metrics in a way that would not manifest at serving time — the model would perform worse in production than on the test set by a margin proportional to how much it relied on that feature. Issues 2 and 3 are structural label-construction problems that affect training signal quality. Issue 4 is a known limitation that the design inadvertently exacerbates by explicitly testing on new-content generalization.

issues_raised: The session-level engagement aggregate may encode current-session dwell/resolution signal that constitutes label leakage and will be unavailable at serving time.
Article-level historical engagement metrics (CTR, resolution rate) may be computed globally rather than temporally bounded per split, leaking future engagement data into earlier windows.
The no-ticket resolution signal is session-level but attributed per-article, causing label ambiguity in multi-article sessions that conflates co-occurrence with resolution with causation.

The DistilBERT baseline is evaluated on behavioral labels it was not optimized for, making it impossible to determine whether the proposed model's gain comes from behavioral features or from label-metric alignment artifacts.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a fairly well-designed experiment. The temporal split is correct, preprocessing is scoped to training data, and the composite label explicitly addresses the click-as-ground-truth problem. I'll focus on the structural issues I can identify, rather than manufacturing concerns.

---

### Issue 1: The session-level engagement aggregate is a leakage vector

**The claim:** The 'session-level engagement aggregate summarizing article dwell and scroll activity within the current session' is a legitimate interaction feature for ranking.

**Why this might be wrong:** This feature aggregates dwell and scroll *within the current session* — but the label (dwell ≥ 90s, no ticket in 24h) is also derived from the current session. If the aggregate includes dwell time on the article being scored, this is direct label leakage: you are using the outcome to predict the outcome. Even if it aggregates over *other* articles in the session, it still encodes session-level engagement depth that correlates strongly with whether the user resolved their issue — which is exactly what the label captures. This creates a circularity that will inflate NDCG@5 in evaluation but will not be available in the deployment scenario where ranking must happen before the session concludes.

**Evidence:** Ablate the model with vs. without the session-level engagement aggregate. If NDCG@5 drops substantially on the held-out test set when the feature is removed, that is strong evidence of leakage or at minimum a feature that is only available post-hoc. Also check whether the feature is available at serving time — if the ranker runs before the user has dwell data, the feature is definitionally unavailable.

---

### Issue 2: Historical engagement metrics on articles computed from 'prior months' are not properly temporally bounded

**The claim:** Article historical click-through rate and historical resolution rate are computed from prior months' data and are safe to use as features.

**Why this might be wrong:** The methodology says these are 'computed from prior months' data' but does not specify whether this boundary is re-calculated per split. For articles in the validation set (month 4), their historical CTR should only include data from months 1–3. For articles in the test set (months 5–6), it should only include months 1–4. If instead a single global historical engagement metric is computed over all 6 months and then used as a feature uniformly, future engagement data leaks into earlier splits. This is a subtle but common mistake — the feature appears 'historical' but is computed with information from the future.

**Evidence:** Audit the code that computes article-level historical features to confirm the cutoff date is evaluated relative to the window boundary, not globally. A synthetic check: create an article with zero clicks in months 1–4 and high clicks in months 5–6. Its historical CTR in the training set should be zero. If it is not, the feature is leaking.

---

### Issue 3: The composite label conflates resolution with article quality for multi-article sessions

**The claim:** The composite score (click + dwell + no ticket) is a valid per-article relevance signal.

**Why this might be wrong:** The 'no support ticket opened within 24 hours' signal is session-level, not article-level. In a session where a user reads three articles and resolves their issue, all three articles receive the +1 resolution credit. But only one (or zero) of them actually caused resolution. This means the label does not rank articles by their individual contribution to resolution — it ranks them by co-occurrence with resolution. For LightGBM's pairwise/listwise objective, this creates label noise that may cause the model to learn that 'articles appearing in successful sessions' are good, not 'articles that caused sessions to succeed.' This degrades the ranking signal specifically for the multi-article case, which is likely the harder, more important case.

**Evidence:** Stratify model performance (NDCG@5) by session length (number of articles viewed). If performance degrades on multi-article sessions relative to single-article sessions, the label ambiguity is the probable cause. Separately, the held-out manually labeled subset should specifically include multi-article sessions, and inter-annotator agreement on those cases would reveal whether human judges can resolve what the implicit label cannot.

---

### Issue 4: The DistilBERT baseline is evaluated on a metric it was not designed to optimize, making the comparison potentially uninformative

**The claim:** Evaluating the DistilBERT baseline on the same NDCG@5 metric as the proposed model ensures the comparison 'isolates behavioral features and ranking optimization.'

**Why this might be wrong:** DistilBERT semantic retrieval using cosine similarity produces a relevance ordering over articles. But the NDCG@5 labels are composite behavioral scores, not semantic relevance scores. DistilBERT was trained on semantic similarity; the labels reward behavioral outcomes. The baseline will underperform not because behavioral features are better, but because cosine similarity does not correlate with the composite label even when it correctly identifies semantically relevant articles. This means the experiment cannot distinguish between two hypotheses: (A) behavioral features add signal beyond semantics, and (B) the label operationalization favors clicked-and-dwelt articles which are ranked higher by any position-aware signal. A fair comparison requires either a DistilBERT model fine-tuned on the composite labels or at minimum a BM25 baseline that has no semantic signal — to triangulate where the gain actually comes from.

**Evidence:** Add a BM25 ranker as a second baseline. If BM25 outperforms DistilBERT on NDCG@5, that suggests the label operationalization favors lexical matching over semantics, and the proposed model's gain over DistilBERT may be partially an artifact of the label. If DistilBERT and BM25 perform comparably, that isolates behavioral features as the explanatory variable.

---

### Summary Assessment

Issues 1 and 3 are the most structurally serious. Issue 1 (session engagement leakage) could produce a model that appears to work in offline evaluation but degrades at serving time because the feature is unavailable. Issue 3 (label conflation in multi-article sessions) undermines the core claim that the composite label captures article-level resolution quality. Issue 2 is a common subtle leakage pattern worth auditing. Issue 4 weakens the conclusions that can be drawn from the comparison but does not invalidate the model itself.
